does: This paper applies sparse autoencoders (SAEs) to interpret embeddings from gene expression models, extending their use beyond large language models into biology. The authors validate SAEs on simulated data to assess their ability to recover ground truth generative variables, systematically characterizing their efficacy across hyperparameter choices and identifying fundamental limitations. They then apply SAEs to embeddings from pretrained single-cell RNA-seq models and demonstrate that SAE features can isolate key biological processes and detect subtle signals in complex high-dimensional biological data. The work introduces scFeatureLens, an automated method that links discovered SAE features to known gene sets and biological concepts, enabling systematic feature interpretation and hypothesis generation at scale.
connects: This work bridges interpretability methods from mechanistic studies of large language models with challenges in computational biology. SAEs emerge as a response to limitations of dense latent representations in interpreting neural network embeddings—prior work developed SAEs specifically to disentangle features in language models by projecting into high-dimensional sparse spaces. The paper extends this interpretability framework to single-cell genomics, building on a growing body of work applying deep learning to gene expression data and the broader need to understand what biological signals neural networks capture. It responds to the challenge of feature attribution in complex biological models by adopting automated discovery approaches rather than post-hoc explanation methods common in computational biology.
enables: This work opens the path for using SAEs as a general interpretability tool in genomics and other high-dimensional biological domains beyond single-cell RNA-seq. By validating SAEs on ground truth biological data and providing systematic hyperparameter guidance, it enables practitioners to apply the method with confidence and understand when it will succeed or fail. The scFeatureLens framework points toward automated pipelines that continuously link machine learning discoveries to biological knowledge bases, potentially accelerating hypothesis generation in genomics research. Future work could extend SAE-based interpretation to multimodal biological data, integrate SAE features with causal inference methods, or combine SAE decomposition with perturbation experiments to validate discovered features as mechanistic drivers of cellular phenotypes.
